title: Revert bridge.ts→claudeStore import, restore standalone ClaudeMessageContext
analysis:
bridge.ts (wire/transport layer) currently imports `MessageContext` from claudeStore.ts (domain/state layer) at line 8, then defines `ClaudeMessageContext = Pick<MessageContext, 3 fields>` at line 115. This inverts the natural layering: sidecar/types → bridge.ts → claudeStore.ts → App.tsx. The import makes the wire layer depend on the domain layer.

The fix is straightforward: (1) replace the Pick type alias with a standalone interface having the same 3 optional fields, (2) remove the claudeStore import, (3) add a comment pointing to types.typecheck.ts as the drift guard.

For types.typecheck.ts, the current bidirectional assignability assertions (`_fwd`/`_rev`) are tautological because `ClaudeMessageContext` IS literally `Pick<MessageContext, ...>` — they can never fail. After reverting to standalone, they recover meaning but remain weak due to the all-optional field problem: since all 3 fields (`selectedEntity?`, `diagnostics?`, `constraints?`) are optional, `{}` satisfies both types, making assignability pass even if field names diverge entirely. The fix is to use TypeScript's mapped-type equality pattern (`Equals<A,B>`) which compares exact structural shape, not just assignability.

App.tsx (line 65-68) already manually maps `context.selectedEntity/diagnostics/constraints` when calling `claudeSendMessage`, so no changes needed there. ChatPanel.tsx works with `MessageContext` from the store, not `ClaudeMessageContext`. The existing runtime tests in claudeBridge.test.ts test the actual invoke behavior and don't depend on the type definition mechanism.

prerequisites.description: Verify current codebase compiles: run `npm run typecheck` in gui/ to establish green baseline before any changes

steps.description: Update types.typecheck.ts: Replace weak bidirectional assignability assertions (_fwd/_rev) with an Equals<A,B> mapped-type utility that checks exact structural equality. This catches field renames, additions, and removals even when all fields are optional. Add a comment documenting that the all-optional weakness exists for assignability but is mitigated by the Equals check. The expected type remains `Pick<MessageContext, 'selectedEntity' | 'diagnostics' | 'constraints'>`. This should compile now (types are currently identical) and will continue to compile after the standalone revert.

Revert bridge.ts: (a) Remove `import type { MessageContext } from './stores/claudeStore'` from line 8. (b) Replace `export type ClaudeMessageContext = Pick<MessageContext, ...>` (line 115) with a standalone interface: `export interface ClaudeMessageContext { selectedEntity?: string; diagnostics?: string[]; constraints?: string[]; }`. (c) Add a JSDoc comment above the interface pointing to `__tests__/types.typecheck.ts` as the structural drift guard that keeps this in sync with MessageContext. The claudeSendMessage function signature and body remain unchanged.

Verify everything compiles and tests pass: run `npm run typecheck` (tsc --noEmit) to confirm the standalone interface stays in sync via the Equals assertion, then run `npm test` to confirm runtime behavior is unchanged (claudeBridge.test.ts, bridge.test.ts, etc.).

design_decisions.decision: Use Equals<A,B> mapped-type equality instead of bidirectional assignability
design_decisions.rationale: Bidirectional assignability (`const _fwd: A = {} as B; const _rev: B = {} as A`) is defeated by all-optional types — `{}` satisfies any type with only optional fields, so field renames or entirely different field sets would pass the check. The `Equals<A,B>` pattern using `(<T>() => T extends A ? 1 : 2) extends (<T>() => T extends B ? 1 : 2)` compares exact type identity, catching any structural divergence.

design_decisions.decision: Standalone interface rather than type alias
design_decisions.rationale: Using `export interface ClaudeMessageContext { ... }` rather than `export type ClaudeMessageContext = { ... }` follows the project convention (claudeStore.ts uses interfaces for MessageContext, AssistantMessage, etc.) and provides better error messages when the typecheck assertions fail.

The expected type in the drift guard should still be derived from MessageContext via Pick — this is the source of truth for what fields bridge.ts SHOULD have. If MessageContext adds/renames/removes one of the 3 fields, the Pick will catch it at compile time, and the Equals assertion will fail if bridge.ts hasn't been updated to match.

design_decisions.decision: No changes to App.tsx, ChatPanel.tsx, or claudeStore.ts
design_decisions.rationale: App.tsx already manually maps MessageContext fields to ClaudeMessageContext when calling claudeSendMessage (lines 65-68). ChatPanel.tsx works with MessageContext from the store. The reconciliation point (App.tsx) correctly bridges domain→wire types. No coupling changes needed outside bridge.ts and the typecheck file.

reuse.how: Extend the existing structural sync guard section (lines 116-127) with the stronger Equals<A,B> assertion, replacing the weak _fwd/_rev assignability checks.

reuse.what: MessageContext interface as source of truth
reuse.how: The expected type in typecheck.ts (`_ExpectedClaudeContext`) continues to derive from `Pick<MessageContext, ...>`, keeping claudeStore.ts as the single source of truth for field names and types.

These tests verify claudeSendMessage behavior with context objects. They don't depend on how ClaudeMessageContext is defined (standalone vs Pick), so they serve as regression tests confirming the refactoring doesn't break runtime behavior.
